author: @Markle2k
text: You need a sliding seat to get more power out of your legs. Also, some better gearing.

replies.author: @dolanbaker
replies.text: I think the issue would be that there would be too much pressure on the pedals, which are now being used for steering, one good pull and they might snap.  You might need to rethink the steering and use the pedals as the solid stop for your rowing with the sliding seat. Perhaps fit a tiller above the handle bar.

author: @wayne1959
text: certainly I enjoyed the video Rob..i live you getting chuffed when a plan comes together..it reminded me of me years ago..keep em coming mate

author: @Sailer2

author: @vitordelima
text: Rowing can make a massive amount of continuous power, there were electric cars in the past powered by rowing.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Lmao, really funny. Comedy aside however, I really think that this has potential. Imagine if you had a multi-gear cog like on a racing cycle. I wonder if it's possible to store the energy from the rowing into a system that allows you to pause and stay in motion or even just to continue motion when you are retracting the cable ready for the next row (pull) motion. This would allow for continual smooth movement rather than a jerking movement.

author: @timothygorman7528
text:
Ok.  First of all, I was waiting for LUKE to give it a go.

Secondly, I think you're on to something.  Why isn't "rowing" or arm and upper body strength more capable than pedaling?  Of course, it does take away the ability to steer and things like this.  

I still wanted to see LUKE figuring it all out though, ha ha :)

author: @olivierroy1301
text: A few years ago I made all my displacements on a bicycle. Then one day I bought an electric motor wheel for it. Then I realised I didn't need an electric motor, so I sold it and took care of myself instead, so I can get around town.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: It's nice to have an electric motor if you live in a hilly area, you use your bike as a commuter to work, etc.  If it's just for fun and/or exercise, yeah, I agree, better to use as is.

author: @markmetzger5430
text: Good day Robbert,  pretty underwhelming by the looks of things....which is entirely not the point :) I applaud your pluck ,  tenacity and ingenuity in application. I find the new skills learned, the problem solving eureka moments and the shear joy of building anything at all, the true joy in these endeavors. Your child like curiosity and genuine jolly nature are contagious to say the least. I am, as the other gentlemen mentioned, sparked by all of your projects. Nice work mate!

author: @TerryGilsenan
text:
Thats excellent. A couple of suggestions: 1, add a rotary spring and ratchet to the primary pulley to act as a kind of accumulator that takes the pulsed power input and releases it over a longer period. 2, add some chain tensioners and a ratchet to get rid of the backlash that is visible in the chain drive assembly.

3, then get Luke to drive it while you have a coffee and laugh :)
